text: Hello and welcome back to Wired Workshop! My name is Gemini, and in our last session, we opened the door to the control room of the network. We learned that Cisco devices run a powerful, specialized operating system called the IOS, and we discovered the tools we need—a console cable and a terminal emulator like PuTTY—to make first contact and access its Command-Line Interface.

So, you've opened the door, and you're now standing inside, face-to-face with a blinking cursor. What now? It might seem intimidating, but what you're looking at is the most powerful and precise interface for network administration in the world. However, the Cisco IOS is not just one big, flat command prompt where you can type anything you want. It has a very strict, hierarchical structure of different modes. Each mode is like a different room or a different security level within the device's operating system, and each one gives you access to a specific set of commands and capabilities.

Think of the IOS as a highly secure, multi-level government building that houses all the controls for our digital city. You can't just walk in off the street and start rewriting the laws. You have to follow a specific path. You start in the lobby, and from there, with the right credentials, you might be allowed into a general security office. Only with special clearance can you enter the secure rooms where the actual blueprints are changed.

Today, we are going to learn how to walk around inside this building. We will explore the primary command modes that form the foundation of the IOS. We'll then go deeper into the powerful configuration modes where you can make changes to the device. And finally, we'll learn the exact commands you need to navigate between these different levels with confidence. This is the rules of the road for the CLI, and by the end of this lesson, you'll be able to navigate the Cisco IOS like a pro.
The Two Primary Modes: User EXEC and Privileged EXEC. When you first connect to a Cisco device, you will always start at the most basic level. The IOS has two primary modes of operation.

Let's start with the first one you'll see: User EXEC Mode. This is the entry-level mode. It's a very low-privilege, view-only environment that is designed to be a safe starting point. The easiest way to know you're in User EXEC Mode is by looking at the prompt. The command-line prompt will be the name of the device, followed by a greater-than symbol. For a brand-new switch, it would simply look like this: Switch>. That greater-than symbol is your visual cue. It's the IOS telling you, You are currently at the lowest privilege level.

Using our government building analogy, User EXEC Mode is the lobby. When you're in the lobby, your capabilities are very limited. You can look at the building directory to see some basic information, and you can perform a few simple tests. For example, you can use basic commands like ping to see if you can reach another device on the network, or traceroute to trace the path your data takes to a destination. However, you cannot view the device's configuration file, you can't restart the device, and you absolutely cannot make any changes to its settings. It's a safe sandbox for performing very limited, non-disruptive tests.

To do any real administrative work, you need to elevate your privileges. This brings us to the second primary mode: Privileged EXEC Mode. This is the central administrative mode for managing and monitoring the device. From here, you have access to every single command that shows you the status of the device, allows you to debug problems, and lets you manage the device's files. While you can't change the configuration from here, you can see everything.

To move from the lobby (User EXEC Mode) into this more powerful mode, you use the enable command. When you type enable and press Enter, you are requesting a higher level of access. If a password has been set, the device will prompt you for it.

Once you're in, you will see a critical change in the prompt. The greater-than symbol > will change to a hash or pound symbol #. For our switch, it would now look like this: Switch#. This hash symbol is your new visual cue, and it's one of the most important things to recognize. It is the IOS telling you, You now have elevated privileges. The commands you type here are powerful.

Using our analogy, the enable command is like showing your security credentials to the guard in the lobby. You've been verified, and you are now granted access to the main security office. From this office, you have access to all the surveillance cameras in the building—this is where you can use the powerful show commands to view every detail of the device's configuration and status. You can see the full running configuration, the status of every port, the IP routing table, and much more. From this security office, you also have the power to perform high-level administrative tasks, like telling the entire device to reload, which means reboot. You can also save the current configuration, manage software images, and troubleshoot complex network problems. Privileged EXEC mode is your all-seeing eye into the device's operations.

Making Changes: Configuration Mode and Its Submodes. So, we can see everything from Privileged EXEC mode, but what if we want to actually change something? What if we want to change the device's name, set a password, or configure a port? For that, we need to enter an even higher level of hierarchy: Global Configuration Mode.

To get there, you must first be in Privileged EXEC Mode (with the hash prompt). From there, you type the command configure terminal. A common shortcut that you will see network engineers use is conf t. When you enter this command, you are telling the device, I am about to make changes that will affect the entire device.

Once again, the prompt will change to give you a clear visual cue of your location. It will now have the word (config) inserted before the hash symbol, like this: Switch(config)#. This prompt tells you that you are in Global Configuration Mode, and the commands you type now will alter the device's live, running configuration.

In our analogy, this is the master blueprint room. Any decision you make in this room applies to the entire building. From here, you can use commands to change the building's name, which we call the hostname. You can set the main security passwords that protect access to privileged mode. You can configure DNS settings, or create banners that display a legal warning to anyone who logs in. These are global, device-wide settings.

However, most of our configuration work is far more specific. We don't just want to change the building's name; we want to configure a specific door, a specific window, or a specific security camera. For these tasks, we need to move from the general blueprint room into more specialized Subconfiguration Modes.

From Global Configuration Mode, you can enter a variety of submodes, each one dedicated to configuring a specific feature or component of the device. Think of these as the individual drafting tables or workstations inside the main blueprint room. To work on the electrical plan, you go to the electrical table. To work on the plumbing plan, you go to the plumbing table.

Let's look at the two most common submodes. The first is Interface Mode. You enter this mode to configure a specific port, or interface, on a switch or router. From global config mode, you would type a command like interface FastEthernet0/1. Notice what happens to the prompt: it changes again, to Switch(config-if)#. The if stands for interface. Now, you are in a specific context. The commands available to you are only the ones relevant to configuring an interface, like setting an IP address, adding a description, or changing the speed.

The second common submode is Line Mode. You enter this mode to configure the various management lines, like the console port or the virtual lines used for remote SSH and Telnet access. From global config, you would type a command like line console 0 to configure the console port. The prompt would change to Switch(config-line)#. From here, you can set a password that a user must provide to access the device via the console cable.

The key takeaway is that the IOS is context-sensitive. The commands you can type are dependent on the mode you are in. This hierarchical structure provides organization and prevents you from accidentally making a change in the wrong place. You cannot set an IP address from global config mode; you must first move into the specific interface mode for the port you want to configure.
Finding Your Way: The Navigation Commands. Now that we understand the different rooms in our building, how do we walk between them? There is a simple and logical set of commands for navigation.
To move up the hierarchy, meaning to a more privileged or more specific mode, you use a series of enabling commands.
To go from User EXEC to Privileged EXEC, you use the enable command.
To go from Privileged EXEC to Global Configuration mode, you use the configure terminal command.
And to go from Global Config to a submode, you use a specific command like interface or line.
To move down the hierarchy, meaning to a less privileged or more general mode, the primary command is exit.
If you are in Interface Mode, typing exit will take you one step back, to Global Configuration Mode.
If you are in Global Configuration Mode, typing exit will take you one step back, to Privileged EXEC Mode.
If you want to leave privileged mode and return to the basic User EXEC mode, you can use the disable command.

This step-by-step movement is logical, but sometimes you might be deep inside a configuration submode and you want to quickly get all the way back to the main security office, Privileged EXEC mode, without typing exit multiple times. For this, the IOS gives you a teleporter command: end. Typing the end command, or using the keyboard shortcut Control-Z, will immediately jump you from any level of configuration mode directly back to the Privileged EXEC prompt. It's an incredibly useful shortcut that you will use all the time.

Think of it this way: exit is like walking out of an office and back into the main hallway. end is like an express elevator that takes you from wherever you are, no matter how deep inside the building, straight back to the high-level security office where you can issue show commands and verify the changes you just made.

Let's recap our grand tour of the Cisco IOS. We started our journey in the lobby, or User EXEC Mode, identified by its greater-than prompt. We used the enable command to enter the security office, or Privileged EXEC Mode, identified by its powerful hash prompt. From there, we used configure terminal to step into the master blueprint room, Global Configuration Mode, where we saw the (config)# prompt. We then dove deeper into specific offices, like Interface Mode and Line Mode, to configure individual components. And we learned how to navigate this hierarchy, using exit to move back one step at a time, and end to instantly return to Privileged EXEC Mode.

This hierarchical structure is one of the most fundamental concepts you will learn in your networking career. It provides a logical, organized, and secure way to manage a complex device. Understanding how to navigate these modes is the first and most critical step to truly mastering the command-line interface.

Now that we know our way around, our next step is to learn the language. In our next lesson, we'll explore the basic structure of Cisco IOS commands, how to use the built-in help features when you're stuck, and how to perform the most essential initial configuration settings on a brand-new device. You won't want to miss it.

Thanks for joining me today at Wired Workshop. If this video helped you understand how to navigate the Cisco IOS, be sure to hit that like button and subscribe for more. As always, any questions, drop them in the comments below. Until next time, keep learning!
